translatedText: Participation and leadership in the Donghak Peasant Revolution
Participated in the Donghak Peasant Revolution in 1894 and commanded the Donghak army at Palbong, Haeju.
Chihapo Incident
In 1896, he assassinated a Japanese man, Tsuchida Josuke, in Chihapo, Hwanghae-do, to avenge the assassination of Empress Myeongseong, the Mother of the Nation.
Patriotic Enlightenment Movement and Shinminhoe activities

In the early 1900s, he devoted himself to educational activities such as establishing schools, and in 1907, he served as the general director of the Hwanghae-do branch of Shinminhoe, a secret society for the national sovereignty restoration movement.
Served in key positions in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea

He served in key positions in the Provisional Government, including a member of the Provisional Assembly, Director of the Police Bureau, Minister of Home Affairs, Premier, and President, defending the legitimacy of the Provisional Government since 1919.
Organization of the Korean Patriotic Organization and command of anti-Japanese uprisings
He organized the Korean Patriotic Organization in 1931 and directed Lee Bong-chang's Tokyo uprising and Yun Bong-gil's Hongkou Park uprising.
Founding and commanding the Korean Liberation Army

In 1940, he organized the Korean Liberation Army, the first official military force of the Provisional Government, and took office as the Chairman of the Supreme Command of the Liberation Army in 1944, leading the forces.
Enactment of the founding principles of the Republic of Korea and declaration of war against Japan
He proclaimed the founding principles of the Republic of Korea in 1941 and issued a declaration of war against Japan, manifesting the will for independence.
Participation in North-South negotiations and dedication to youth education
He visited Pyongyang in 1948 to participate in North-South negotiations for the establishment of a unified government, and subsequently devoted himself to youth education.
What are Kim Gu's real name and pen name?
His first name was Chang-am, changed to Chang-su at the age of 19, and to Gu (from 龜 to 九) at the age of 36 (1912). His pen names are Baekbeom and Yeonsang.
How was the pen name 'Baekbeom' created?
The pen name 'Baekbeom' was created by taking 'Baek (白)' from baekjeong, symbolizing the humble commoners, and 'Beom (凡)' from beombu, meaning ordinary people.
Who was the person Kim Gu assassinated in the Chihapo Incident, and what was the reason?
Kim Gu assassinated a Japanese man named Tsuchida Josuke in Chihapo, Hwanghae-do in 1896, stating the reason for the murder was to avenge the death of Empress Myeongseong, the Mother of the Nation.
What were the major positions Kim Gu held in the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea?

In the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea, Kim Gu served as a member of the Assembly, Director of the Police Bureau, Minister of Home Affairs, Acting Prime Minister, Premier, State Council Member, and Chairman (President) of the State Council of the Provisional Government from 1940 to 1947.
What is the representative independence movement organization founded by Kim Gu?
Kim Gu organized the Korean Patriotic Organization, an independence movement group, in 1931 and directed the uprisings of Lee Bong-chang and Yun Bong-gil.
When and where did Kim Gu pass away?
Kim Gu passed away on June 26, 1949, assassinated by a bullet fired by Ahn Doo-hee at his residence, Gyeonggyojang.
Preaching the thought of becoming a Buddha (Seongbul)
Gautama Buddha preached that anyone can become a Buddha, and this process of becoming a Buddha is called Seongbul (Attaining Buddhahood).
What does Buddha mean?
Buddha means 'the awakened one' or 'the enlightened one' in Sanskrit, and is a term used in Buddhism to refer to a person who has attained enlightenment.
Who is usually called Buddha?
Anyone who is awakened can be called a Buddha, but it usually refers to Gautama Buddha.
What is the etymology of the word Buddha?
The word Buddha came from the Sanskrit word 'Buddha' and entered through the Chinese characters 'Bulche (佛體)' into Korean.
Born on October 15 in Röcken, Saxony, Prussia, as the son of a Lutheran pastor.
His younger sister, Elisabeth Nietzsche, was born.
His younger brother, Ludwig Joseph, was born.
His father, Carl Wilhelm Ludwig Nietzsche, passed away from a brain disease.
His younger brother died.
Began attending a boys' school.
Started attending the Domgymnasium in Naumburg.
After his grandmother passed away, the family moved into their own house.
Enrolled in Schulpforta and continued his studies there until 1864.
After graduating from Schulpforta, he began studying theology and classical philosophy at the University of Bonn.
Discovered the writings of Schopenhauer and transferred to the University of Leipzig in October following Ritschl.
Read Friedrich Albert Lange's book, 'History of Materialism and Critique of its Present Importance'.
Volunteered for the military and enlisted in October; wrote 'Zur Geschichte der Theognideischen Spruchsammlung'.
In March, suffered a horseback riding accident that left him unable to continue military service, and began meeting the musician Richard Wagner.

Appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland, and received his doctorate from the University of Leipzig without an examination or dissertation. Wrote 'Homer and Classical Philology'.

His health further deteriorated, and he wrote 'Analecta Laertiana', 'Der florentinische Tractat über Homer und Hesiod', 'The Greek Music Drama', 'Philosophy in the Tragic Age of the Greeks', and 'Socrates and Tragedy'.
Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'.
Began collecting posthumous records such as Philosophy in the Tragic Age of the Greeks.
Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'.
Formed a friendship with Paul Rée, and distanced himself from Wagner after being disappointed at the Bayreuth Festival.
Published 'Human, All Too Human'.
Resigned from his professorship at the University of Basel due to declining health.
Abandoned plans to travel to Tunis, and received the inspiration for the idea of eternal recurrence in Sils Maria. Wrote 'The Dawn'.
Wrote 'The Gay Science'.
Wrote 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.
Wrote 'Beyond Good and Evil'.
Wrote 'On the Genealogy of Morality'.
Wrote 'The Case of Wagner', 'Twilight of the Idols', 'The Antichrist', 'Ecce Homo', 'Dionysian Dithyrambs', and 'Nietzsche contra Wagner'.
Collapsed in Turin, Italy in January and was admitted to an asylum, becoming mentally incapacitated.
Died in Weimar on August 25.
'The Will to Power', a posthumous compilation of Nietzsche's writings, was published.
The copyright period for Nietzsche's works expired.
Philosopher and writer
As a German philosopher and writer, he sought to break Western tradition and establish new values.
Acquired the nickname 'Philosopher with a Hammer'
Because he sought to shatter Western tradition and establish new values, he earned the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer'.
Research on the origins of Christian morality and rationalism
He dedicated himself to uncovering the origins of Christian morality and rationalism, arguing that rational things originated from irrationality and madness.
Advocated 'loving life on earth'
Opposing the idealist dichotomy, he advocated for loving one's life in reality.
Critique of totalitarianism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism
He spread thoughts criticizing totalitarianism, nationalism, statism, and anti-Semitism.
Appointed professor of classical philology at the University of Basel
He became a professor of classical philology at the University of Basel in Switzerland at the age of 24.
Obtained doctorate degree
Received his doctorate from the University of Leipzig in 1869 based solely on his published writings.
Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy'
Wrote 'The Birth of Tragedy' in 1872, containing an original aesthetic theory on ancient Greek tragedy.
Wrote 'Untimely Meditations'
Between 1873 and 1876, he wrote 'Untimely Meditations', containing cultural critiques challenging the development of German culture.
Published 'Human, All Too Human'
In 1878, he published 'Human, All Too Human', full of his characteristic aphorisms, beginning his departure from the philosophies of Schopenhauer and Wagner.
Presented the concept of the 'Death of God'
Presented the concept of the 'death of God', signifying the loss of the highest values created by humans and the advent of nihilism.
Advocated Monism (Ontology of Life)
Advocated for monism centering on the will to power, opposing the dualism of modern philosophy, and breaking away from the distinction between subject and object.
Distinguished concepts of nihilism
Divided nihilism into active nihilism and passive nihilism, emphasizing the importance of an attitude that creates its own meaning.
Presented the thought of 'Eternal Recurrence'
Inspired in Sils Maria in 1881, he presented the thought of 'eternal recurrence' as the fundamental idea in 'Thus Spoke Zarathustra'.
Presented the concept of the 'Übermensch'
Envisioned the 'Übermensch', a human type that overcomes and creates beyond modern values, morality, and faith.
Presented the concept of the 'Will to Power'
Presented the 'will to power' as a concept opposed to the instinct of self-preservation, arguing that the purpose of life is not survival but the manifestation of power.
Critique of morality
Classified morality into 'slave morality' and 'master morality', and criticized it by exploring its genealogy from psychological and axiological perspectives.
Contributions as a classical philologist
Influenced modern aesthetic theory based on his deep understanding of ancient Greece and Rome.
Profound posthumous influence on European philosophy and literature
Although ignored during his lifetime, he influenced numerous thinkers and writers posthumously, including Heidegger, Jaspers, Freud, and Thomas Mann.
Musical compositions
Composed songs and piano pieces from the age of 13 to around 20, and left behind several compositions during his time in Basel.
Why was Nietzsche called the 'philosopher with a hammer'?
Because he sought to shatter Western tradition and establish new values, he was given the nickname 'philosopher with a hammer'.
What was the cause of Nietzsche's mental illness?
He suffered a mental breakdown in his later years. The story that it was due to syphilis is merely a rumor, and according to a report in The Telegraph, it was due to a malignant brain tumor.
Was Nietzsche anti-Semitic?
Although Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, nationalism, statism, and anti-Semitism, his book 'The Antichrist' explicitly contains content that provides a basis for anti-Semitism.
Is Nietzsche's philosophy related to Nazism?

Nietzsche criticized totalitarianism, nationalism, and anti-Semitism, but after his death, his sister Elisabeth distorted his works to favor the Nazi regime. However, it has also been pointed out that his officially published books were utilized by the Nazis as well.
How was Nietzsche evaluated during his lifetime?
During his lifetime, he was thoroughly ignored by academia, harshly criticized by religious circles and moralists, and plagued by slander that he was corrupting society.
